Linear Model with R: Chapter 5

Author

Han Chenyue

Chapter 5

Exercises

Q1

q1a

Show the code 
data(prostate, package = "faraway")
lm_q1a <- lm(lpsa ~ ., data = prostate)
summary(lm_q1a)

Call:
lm(formula = lpsa ~ ., data = prostate)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.7331 -0.3713 -0.0170  0.4141  1.6381 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  0.669337   1.296387   0.516  0.60693    
lcavol       0.587022   0.087920   6.677 2.11e-09 ***
lweight      0.454467   0.170012   2.673  0.00896 ** 
age         -0.019637   0.011173  -1.758  0.08229 .  
lbph         0.107054   0.058449   1.832  0.07040 .  
svi          0.766157   0.244309   3.136  0.00233 ** 
lcp         -0.105474   0.091013  -1.159  0.24964    
gleason      0.045142   0.157465   0.287  0.77503    
pgg45        0.004525   0.004421   1.024  0.30886    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.7084 on 88 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6548,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6234 
F-statistic: 20.86 on 8 and 88 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
Show the code 
new_patient_a = data.frame(
  lcavol = 1.44692,
  lweight = 3.62301,
  age = 65.00000,
  lbph = 0.30010,
  svi = 0.00000,
  lcp = -0.79851,
  gleason = 7.00000,
  pgg45 = 15.00000
)

predict(lm_q1a, newdata = new_patient_a, interval = "prediction")
       fit       lwr      upr
1 2.389053 0.9646584 3.813447

Manual calculations for sanity check:

To compute the CI manually, we will need:

\[ \hat{y}_0 \pm t^{(\alpha / 2)}_{n-p} \hat{\sigma} \sqrt{1 + x_0^T (X^T X)^{-1} x_0} \]

\[ \hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{RSS}{n - p}} = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{e}^T \hat{e}}{n - p}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \hat{e}_i^2}{n - p}} \]

Show the code 
y <- prostate$lpsa
y
 [1] -0.43078 -0.16252 -0.16252 -0.16252  0.37156  0.76547  0.76547  0.85442
 [9]  1.04732  1.04732  1.26695  1.26695  1.26695  1.34807  1.39872  1.44692
[17]  1.47018  1.49290  1.55814  1.59939  1.63900  1.65823  1.69562  1.71380
[25]  1.73166  1.76644  1.80006  1.81645  1.84845  1.89462  1.92425  2.00821
[33]  2.00821  2.02155  2.04769  2.08567  2.15756  2.19165  2.21375  2.27727
[41]  2.29757  2.30757  2.32728  2.37491  2.52172  2.55334  2.56879  2.56879
[49]  2.59152  2.59152  2.65676  2.67759  2.68444  2.69124  2.70471  2.71800
[57]  2.78809  2.79423  2.80639  2.81241  2.84200  2.85359  2.85359  2.88200
[65]  2.88200  2.88759  2.92047  2.96269  2.96269  2.97298  3.01308  3.03735
[73]  3.05636  3.07501  3.27526  3.33755  3.39283  3.43560  3.45789  3.51304
[81]  3.51601  3.53076  3.56530  3.57094  3.58768  3.63099  3.68009  3.71235
[89]  3.98434  3.99360  4.02981  4.12955  4.38515  4.68444  5.14312  5.47751
[97]  5.58293
Show the code 
X <- model.matrix(lm_q1a)
X
   (Intercept)     lcavol lweight age      lbph svi      lcp gleason pgg45
1            1 -0.5798185  2.7695  50 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0
2            1 -0.9942523  3.3196  58 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0
3            1 -0.5108256  2.6912  74 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       7    20
4            1 -1.2039728  3.2828  58 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0
5            1  0.7514161  3.4324  62 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0
6            1 -1.0498221  3.2288  50 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0

18           1  2.2884862  3.6494  66 -1.386294   0  0.37156       6     0

20           1  0.1823216  3.8254  70  1.658228   0 -1.38629       6     0

22           1  2.0592388  3.5010  60  1.474763   0  1.34807       7    20

40           1  0.7975072  3.0131  56  0.936093   0 -0.16252       7     5
41           1  0.6205765  3.1420  60 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       9    80

64           1  2.0347056  3.9170  66  2.008214   1  2.11021       7    60
65           1  2.0731719  3.6230  64 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0

69           1 -0.4462871  4.4085  69 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0
70           1  1.1939225  4.7804  72  2.326302   0 -0.79851       7     5

80           1  2.7794402  3.8232  63 -1.386294   0  0.37156       7    50
81           1  1.4678743  3.0704  66  0.559616   0  0.22314       7    40
82           1  2.5136561  3.4735  57  0.438255   0  2.32728       7    60
83           1  2.6130067  3.8888  77 -0.527633   1  0.55962       7    30
84           1  2.6775910  3.8384  65  1.115142   0  1.74920       9    70
85           1  1.5623463  3.7099  60  1.695616   0  0.81093       7    30
86           1  3.3028493  3.5190  64 -1.386294   1  2.32728       7    60
87           1  2.0241931  3.7317  58  1.638997   0 -1.38629       6     0
88           1  1.7316555  3.3690  62 -1.386294   1  0.30010       7    30
89           1  2.8075938  4.7181  65 -1.386294   1  2.46385       7    60
90           1  1.5623463  3.6951  76  0.936093   1  0.81093       7    75
91           1  3.2464910  4.1018  68 -1.386294   0 -1.38629       6     0
92           1  2.5329028  3.6776  61  1.348073   1 -1.38629       7    15
93           1  2.8302678  3.8764  68 -1.386294   1  1.32176       7    60
94           1  3.8210036  3.8969  44 -1.386294   1  2.16905       7    40
95           1  2.9074474  3.3962  52 -1.386294   1  2.46385       7    10
96           1  2.8825636  3.7739  68  1.558145   1  1.55814       7    80
97           1  3.4719665  3.9750  68  0.438255   1  2.90417       7    20
attr(,"assign")
[1] 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Show the code 
new_patient_manual_calculations <- data.frame(
  intercept = 1,
  lcavol = 1.44692,
  lweight = 3.62301,
  age = 65.00000,
  lbph = 0.30010,
  svi = 0.00000,
  lcp = -0.79851,
  gleason = 7.00000,
  pgg45 = 15.00000
)

new_patient_manual_calculations
  intercept  lcavol lweight age   lbph svi      lcp gleason pgg45
1         1 1.44692 3.62301  65 0.3001   0 -0.79851       7    15
Show the code 
beta_hat <- solve(t(X) %*% X) %*% t(X) %*% y
x0 <- t(as.matrix(new_patient_manual_calculations))
epsilon <- y - X %*% beta_hat
rss <- t(epsilon) %*% epsilon
n <- nrow(X)
p <- ncol(X)
sigma_hat <- sqrt(rss / (n - p))
Show the code 
library(tidyverse)
── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.2.0
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
Show the code 
y0 <- t(x0) %*% beta_hat
y0
         [,1]
[1,] 2.389053
Show the code 
near(
  y0,
  predict(
    lm_q1a,
    newdata = new_patient_manual_calculations,
    interval = "prediction"
  )[1, "fit"]
)
     [,1]
[1,] TRUE
Show the code 
t_critical <- qt(0.975, df = n - p)
t_critical
[1] 1.98729
Show the code 
lwr <- y0 -
  t_critical * sigma_hat * sqrt(1 + t(x0) %*% solve(t(X) %*% X) %*% x0)
lwr
          [,1]
[1,] 0.9646584
Show the code 
near(
  lwr,
  predict(
    lm_q1a,
    newdata = new_patient_manual_calculations,
    interval = "prediction"
  )[1, "lwr"]
)
     [,1]
[1,] TRUE
Show the code 
upr <- y0 +
  t_critical * sigma_hat * sqrt(1 + t(x0) %*% solve(t(X) %*% X) %*% x0)
upr
         [,1]
[1,] 3.813447
Show the code 
near(
  upr,
  predict(
    lm_q1a,
    newdata = new_patient_manual_calculations,
    interval = "prediction"
  )[1, "upr"]
)
     [,1]
[1,] TRUE

q1b

Show the code 
new_patient_b <- data.frame(
  lcavol = 1.44692,
  lweight = 3.62301,
  age = 20.00000,
  lbph = 0.30010,
  svi = 0.00000,
  lcp = -0.79851,
  gleason = 7.00000,
  pgg45 = 15.00000
)

predict(lm_q1a, newdata = new_patient_b, interval = "prediction")
       fit      lwr      upr
1 3.272726 1.538744 5.006707

The youngest patient in the original dataset is aged forty-one, whereas the current patient is less than half of that age. Therefore, the prediction involves substantial extrapolation beyond the range of the observed data, making it less reliable and resulting in a wider prediction interval. Furthermore, the prediction interval is wider because it accounts for the variability of a future observation through the inclusion of the error term \(\epsilon\), in addition to the uncertainty in the estimation of \(\beta\).

q1c

Show the code 
summary(lm_q1a)

Call:
lm(formula = lpsa ~ ., data = prostate)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.7331 -0.3713 -0.0170  0.4141  1.6381 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  0.669337   1.296387   0.516  0.60693    
lcavol       0.587022   0.087920   6.677 2.11e-09 ***
lweight      0.454467   0.170012   2.673  0.00896 ** 
age         -0.019637   0.011173  -1.758  0.08229 .  
lbph         0.107054   0.058449   1.832  0.07040 .  
svi          0.766157   0.244309   3.136  0.00233 ** 
lcp         -0.105474   0.091013  -1.159  0.24964    
gleason      0.045142   0.157465   0.287  0.77503    
pgg45        0.004525   0.004421   1.024  0.30886    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.7084 on 88 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6548,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6234 
F-statistic: 20.86 on 8 and 88 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Only lcavol, lweight, and svi are significant at the \(5\%\) level.

Show the code 
lm_q1c <- lm(lpsa ~ lcavol + lweight + svi, data = prostate)
summary(lm_q1c)

Call:
lm(formula = lpsa ~ lcavol + lweight + svi, data = prostate)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.72964 -0.45764  0.02812  0.46403  1.57013 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -0.26809    0.54350  -0.493  0.62298    
lcavol       0.55164    0.07467   7.388  6.3e-11 ***
lweight      0.50854    0.15017   3.386  0.00104 ** 
svi          0.66616    0.20978   3.176  0.00203 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.7168 on 93 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6264,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6144 
F-statistic: 51.99 on 3 and 93 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
Show the code 
old_model_for_patient_a <- predict(
  lm_q1a,
  newdata = new_patient_a,
  interval = "prediction"
)
new_model_for_patient_a <- predict(
  lm_q1c,
  newdata = new_patient_a,
  interval = "prediction"
)
Show the code 
cat("Result for patient A (Old Model): ", old_model_for_patient_a, "\n")
Result for patient A (Old Model):  2.389053 0.9646584 3.813447
Show the code 
cat("Result for patient A (New Model): ", new_model_for_patient_a)
Result for patient A (New Model):  2.372534 0.9383436 3.806724

The predicted values are very similar.

Show the code 
old_model_for_patient_b <- predict(
  lm_q1a,
  newdata = new_patient_b,
  interval = "prediction"
)
new_model_for_patient_b <- predict(
  lm_q1c,
  newdata = new_patient_b,
  interval = "prediction"
)
Show the code 
cat("Result for patient B (Old Model): ", old_model_for_patient_b, "\n")
Result for patient B (Old Model):  3.272726 1.538744 5.006707
Show the code 
cat("Result for patient B (New Model): ", new_model_for_patient_b)
Result for patient B (New Model):  2.372534 0.9383436 3.806724

In this case, the results differ more noticeably, particularly with respect to the prediction interval.

Based on these examples alone, we cannot determine which model provides better predictive performance. The reduced model, however, has the practical advantage that it requires only three predictors to produce a prediction. If obtaining the full set of predictors is difficult or costly, this simpler model may therefore be preferable.

Q2

q2a

Show the code 
data(teengamb, package = "faraway")
Show the code 
lm_q2a <- lm(gamble ~ sex + status + income + verbal, data = teengamb)
summary(lm_q2a)

Call:
lm(formula = gamble ~ sex + status + income + verbal, data = teengamb)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-51.082 -11.320  -1.451   9.452  94.252 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  22.55565   17.19680   1.312   0.1968    
sex         -22.11833    8.21111  -2.694   0.0101 *  
status        0.05223    0.28111   0.186   0.8535    
income        4.96198    1.02539   4.839 1.79e-05 ***
verbal       -2.95949    2.17215  -1.362   0.1803    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 22.69 on 42 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5267,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4816 
F-statistic: 11.69 on 4 and 42 DF,  p-value: 1.815e-06
Show the code 
apply(subset(teengamb, sex == 0), 2, mean)
      sex    status    income    verbal    gamble 
 0.000000 52.000000  4.976071  6.821429 29.775000
Show the code 
male_with_average_stats_data <- data.frame(
  sex = 0,
  status = 52,
  income = 4.976071,
  verbal = 6.821429,
  gamble = 29.775000
)
predict(lm_q2a, newdata = male_with_average_stats_data, interval = "prediction")
     fit       lwr      upr
1 29.775 -16.82649 76.37648

The 95% prediction interval is [-16.83, 76.38], though in practical terms, the lower bound would be truncated at zero.

Standard linear regression assumes that our response variable gamble can be any number on the continuous number line, extending to infinity in both the positive and negative directions. The model doesn’t “know” that our data represents money.

Furthermore, our Residual Standard Error is 22.69. That is a pretty large amount of error compared to the predicted value (29.775). Because the error is so large, subtracting it from our predicted value pushes the bottom of the interval way past zero.

q2b

Show the code 
aggregate(teengamb[, -1], list(sex = teengamb$sex), max)
  sex status income verbal gamble
1   0     75     15     10  156.0
2   1     65     10      8   19.6
Show the code 
male_with_maximal_stats_data <- data.frame(
  sex = 0,
  status = 75,
  income = 15,
  verbal = 10
)
Show the code 
predict(lm_q2a, newdata = male_with_maximal_stats_data, interval = "prediction")
       fit      lwr    upr
1 71.30794 17.06588 125.55

This CI is wider, in linear regression, our predictions are always the most accurate and have the tightest confidence/prediction intervals right at the “center of mass” of our data. As we moved away from the average and looked at the extreme edges of our data, the model became less certain (Bowtie effect).

There are also less data at the extremes as there are usually a lot of people in a dataset with “average” incomes and scores but there are very few people who have the absolute maximum income, maximum status, and maximum verbal score all at the same time. Because the model has less data representing these extreme individuals, its prediction carried more uncertainty, forcing the interval to be wider.

q2c

Show the code 
lm_q2c <- lm(sqrt(gamble) ~ sex + status + income + verbal, data = teengamb)
summary(lm_q2c)

Call:
lm(formula = sqrt(gamble) ~ sex + status + income + verbal, data = teengamb)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-4.6606 -1.0961 -0.2564  0.9786  5.4178 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  2.97707    1.57947   1.885  0.06638 .  
sex         -2.04450    0.75416  -2.711  0.00968 ** 
status       0.03688    0.02582   1.428  0.16057    
income       0.47938    0.09418   5.090 7.94e-06 ***
verbal      -0.42360    0.19950  -2.123  0.03967 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.084 on 42 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5646,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5231 
F-statistic: 13.61 on 4 and 42 DF,  p-value: 3.362e-07
Show the code 
# Squaring the prediction is needed to reverse the square root on the response. It also solves the problem of having negative lower bound, as the interval cannot mathematically drop below zero anymore.
predict(
  lm_q2c,
  newdata = male_with_average_stats_data,
  interval = "prediction"
)^2
       fit        lwr      upr
1 19.27805 0.01220654 75.18402

q2d

Show the code 
female_with_custom_stats_data <- data.frame(
  sex = 1,
  status = 20,
  income = 1,
  verbal = 10
)
predict(
  lm_q2c,
  newdata = female_with_custom_stats_data,
  interval = "prediction"
)^2
       fit      lwr      upr
1 4.353398 47.73238 7.485167

lwr \(>\) upr, which is nonsensical.

Without squaring it, the results are:

Show the code 
predict(
  lm_q2c,
  newdata = female_with_custom_stats_data,
  interval = "prediction"
)
       fit       lwr      upr
1 -2.08648 -6.908863 2.735903
  • The fatal flaw is that the model predicted the square root of this girl’s gambling spending to be -2.09, with a lower bound of -6.91.
  • Mathematically, the principal square root of a real number cannot be negative. The model has made an impossible prediction.
  • Because a negative times a negative is a positive, squaring her large negative lower bound (-6.91) turns it into a large positive number (47.73).
  • A monotone mathematical function is one that always goes in the same direction (always up or always down). Squaring is not a monotone function across all real numbers, because it reverses the order of negative values.
  • Therefore, squaring these negative bounds distorted and flipped the interval, showing that this model performs poorly (extrapolates badly) for this specific demographic.

Q3
